era_context: The early 14th century continues with the Yuan Dynasty in China under Ayurbarwada (Emperor Renzong, r. 1311–1320), a period of relative stability and cultural flourishing. In Europe, the Avignon Papacy under Pope John XXII (r. 1316–1334) consolidates papal authority. The Hundred Years' War between England and France is in its early phase. The Ottoman Empire continues its expansion in Anatolia.

events.title: Papal Condemnation of the Spiritual Franciscans
events.description: Pope John XXII issued decrees against the Spiritual Franciscans (Fraticelli), a radical faction within the Franciscan order that advocated absolute poverty and rejected papal authority over the order. The conflict between the papacy and the Spirituals had been building for decades, but John XXII's aggressive stance in 1318 marked an escalation. The pope declared their teachings heretical and moved to suppress the movement, leading to persecution and the flight of many Spirituals to remote areas or into schism.
key_figures: Michael of Cesena (Minister General of the Franciscans, later opponent of the pope)

sources.name: Papal bulls and decrees (Quorundam exigit, issued 1317–1318)

events.certainty_note: The papal condemnation is documented in official papal records and contemporary Franciscan sources.

events.title: Renewal of the Anglo-Scottish Border Conflict
events.description: Following the Scottish victory at Bannockburn (1314), Robert I of Scotland sought to consolidate Scottish independence and expand Scottish territory. In 1318, Scottish forces conducted raids and military operations along the English border, seeking to pressure England into recognizing Scottish independence. These conflicts were part of the broader Wars of Scottish Independence and contributed to the eventual Treaty of Edinburgh-Northampton (1328).
key_figures: Robert I of Scotland (King, 'Robert the Bruce')
events.certainty_note: Border conflicts during this period are well-attested, though specific events for 1318 are not always precisely dated.

events.title: Zhiyuan Era Ends; Yanyou Era Begins in Yuan Dynasty
events.description: The Zhiyuan era (1335–1340 in Western calendar, but the Chinese calendar year 1318 corresponds to the end of Zhiyuan and beginning of Yanyou era under Emperor Renzong). This was a period of relative stability and cultural achievement in the Yuan Dynasty. Emperor Renzong (Ayurbarwada) promoted Confucian learning and restored the civil service examination system, marking a shift toward more traditional Chinese governance within the Mongol-ruled empire.

sources.name: Yuan Shi (Official History of the Yuan Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: Yuan Dynasty records are well-preserved. The era change and Emperor Renzong's reign are firmly established.

events.title: Continued Famine Conditions in Northern Europe
events.description: The Great Famine that began in 1315 continued to affect northern Europe in 1318, though conditions were beginning to improve in some regions. Food prices remained elevated, and populations continued to suffer from malnutrition and disease. The famine's effects on population, economy, and social stability were profound and long-lasting.

sources.name: Historiographic consensus on the Great Famine

events.certainty_note: The persistence of famine conditions is well-documented.

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of the papal condemnation of the Spiritual Franciscans in 1318 is sometimes overstated in terms of its immediate effectiveness. While John XXII did issue decrees, the Spirituals were not immediately suppressed; the conflict continued for decades, and some Spirituals found refuge in other regions or under the protection of secular rulers. The suppression was gradual and incomplete.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1318 is again dominated by Western European sources. Chinese sources are well-preserved but often focus on court and administrative matters. Islamic and African sources are underrepresented. The reliability of dating varies by region and type of event.

graph_edges.note: Papal persecution of Spirituals led to further conflict and the eventual schism of some Franciscan factions
Scottish border conflicts contributed to eventual English recognition of Scottish independence
